title: 06 — Association Rules Conditioner: Soft Diagnostic Prior
text:
Idea: Use co-occurrence patterns mined from training labels to inject a soft diagnostic prior into the prompt at inference time. The prior tells the model which findings are statistically likely given the clinical indication — nudging generation without hard-coding any output.
Mechanism (two-tier): 1. Build a pairwise conditional probability table P(B | A) from training labels (kept as fallback). 2. Build a TF-IDF index over training indications. For each test study, retrieve the k=20 most similar training studies by indication and compute the empirical label prevalence over that neighborhood — a data-driven alternative to hand-coded keyword rules. 3. Primary path: if retrieval similarity ≥ 0.05, inject the neighborhood label distribution: “Clinical context: Cardiomegaly present in 67% of similar cases, Edema in 42%…” 4. Fallback path: if retrieval fails (empty indication or low similarity), scan the indication for keyword triggers and inject matching pairwise association rules. 5. The selected hint is injected into the prompt after the system instruction, before Findings:.
Saves reports/eval_metrics_assoc_rules_{VARIANT}.json for notebook 04 STEP 7.
Execution order across notebooks:

title: 06 — Association Rules Conditioner: Soft Diagnostic Prior
section: STEP 2 — Build association rules from training labels
text:
STEP 2 — Build association rules from training labels
Computes pairwise P(B | A) and lift for all label pairs with prevalence ≥ 1%.
CPU-only — runs immediately without a GPU.


Code
from src.data.labels import CHEXBERT_LABELS

train_label_matrix = train_df[CHEXBERT_LABELS].values.astype(float)
n = len(train_label_matrix)

co_matrix   = (train_label_matrix.T @ train_label_matrix) / n
prevalences = train_label_matrix.mean(axis=0)

rules = []
for i, label_a in enumerate(CHEXBERT_LABELS):
    if prevalences[i] < 0.01: continue
    for j, label_b in enumerate(CHEXBERT_LABELS):
        if i == j or prevalences[j] < 0.01: continue
        p_a  = prevalences[i]
        p_ab = co_matrix[i, j]
        conf = p_ab / p_a
        lift = conf / prevalences[j] if prevalences[j] > 0 else 0
        rules.append({
            'antecedent':   label_a,
            'consequent':   label_b,
            'support':      round(float(p_ab),  4),
            'confidence':   round(float(conf),  4),
            'lift':         round(float(lift),  4),
            'p_antecedent': round(float(p_a),   4),
        })

rules_df = pd.DataFrame(rules).sort_values('confidence', ascending=False)
print(f'Total rules      : {len(rules_df)}')
print(f'conf ≥ 0.30      : {(rules_df.confidence >= 0.30).sum()}')
print(f'conf ≥ 0.30 + lift ≥ 1.5: {((rules_df.confidence >= 0.30) & (rules_df.lift >= 1.5)).sum()}')
print()
display(rules_df[rules_df['confidence'] >= 0.30].head(25))

# ── Heat-map of conditional probabilities ─────────────────────────────────────
labels_active = [l for i, l in enumerate(CHEXBERT_LABELS) if prevalences[i] >= 0.01]
active_idx    = [CHEXBERT_LABELS.index(l) for l in labels_active]
conf_matrix   = np.zeros((len(labels_active), len(labels_active)))
for i, ia in enumerate(active_idx):
    for j, ja in enumerate(active_idx):
        if ia != ja and prevalences[ia] > 0:
            conf_matrix[i, j] = co_matrix[ia, ja] / prevalences[ia]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 10))
im = ax.imshow(conf_matrix, cmap='Blues', vmin=0, vmax=0.7, aspect='auto')
ax.set_xticks(range(len(labels_active)))
ax.set_xticklabels(labels_active, rotation=40, ha='right', fontsize=8)
ax.set_yticks(range(len(labels_active)))
ax.set_yticklabels(labels_active, fontsize=8)
for i in range(len(labels_active)):
    for j in range(len(labels_active)):
        if conf_matrix[i, j] > 0:
            ax.text(j, i, f'{conf_matrix[i,j]:.2f}', ha='center', va='center',
                    fontsize=6, color='white' if conf_matrix[i, j] > 0.4 else 'black')
plt.colorbar(im, ax=ax, shrink=0.8, label='P(consequent | antecedent)')
ax.set_xlabel('Consequent'); ax.set_ylabel('Antecedent')
ax.set_title('Label co-occurrence — P(B | A) from training set')
plt.tight_layout()
fig.savefig(FIGURES_DIR / 'assoc_rules_heatmap.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
print('Saved assoc_rules_heatmap.png'); plt.show()
# ── TF-IDF label prior: retrieve similar studies and compute empirical P(label | indication) ──
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer
from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity as _cosine_sim

_TFIDF_K      = 20
_MIN_SIM_TFIDF = 0.05

train_indications_for_rules = train_df['indication'].fillna('').astype(str).tolist()
_rules_tfidf = TfidfVectorizer(max_features=5000, stop_words='english', ngram_range=(1, 2))
_rules_tfidf_matrix = _rules_tfidf.fit_transform(train_indications_for_rules)
print(f'TF-IDF label-prior index: {_rules_tfidf_matrix.shape[0]} docs × {_rules_tfidf_matrix.shape[1]} features')


def retrieve_label_prior(indication: str, k: int = _TFIDF_K) -> dict[str, float] | None:
    """Retrieve top-k similar training studies and return empirical label prevalence.

    Returns None when indication is empty or max similarity is below threshold.
    """
    if not indication.strip():
        return None
    q = _rules_tfidf.transform([indication])
    sims = _cosine_sim(q, _rules_tfidf_matrix).flatten()
    if sims.max() < _MIN_SIM_TFIDF:
        return None
    top_idx = sims.argsort()[::-1][:k]
    subset  = train_label_matrix[top_idx]          # shape (k, 14)
    priors  = dict(zip(CHEXBERT_LABELS, subset.mean(axis=0)))
    return priors


def build_label_prior_hint(priors: dict[str, float], min_prevalence: float = 0.30) -> str:
    """Format empirical label prevalence from retrieved studies as a clinical context hint."""
    relevant = [(label, p) for label, p in priors.items()
                if p >= min_prevalence and label != 'No Finding']
    if not relevant:
        return ''
    relevant.sort(key=lambda x: -x[1])
    lines = ['Clinical context (based on similar studies in training set):']
    for label, p in relevant:
        lines.append(f'  - {label}: present in {100*p:.0f}% of similar cases.')
    return '\n'.join(lines)

title: 06 — Association Rules Conditioner: Soft Diagnostic Prior
text:
STEP 3 — Prompt conditioner functions
Builds the soft diagnostic prior from the rules table and injects it into the prompt.


Code
_INDICATION_KEYWORDS = {
    'Enlarged Cardiomediastinum': ['mediastinal widening', 'mediastinum', 'widened mediastinum'],
    'Cardiomegaly':     ['cardio', 'cardiac', 'heart', 'cardiomeg'],
    'Lung Opacity':     ['opacity', 'infiltrate', 'airspace disease', 'haziness'],
    'Lung Lesion':      ['nodule', 'mass', 'lesion', 'lung lesion', 'pulmonary nodule'],
    'Edema':            ['edema', 'pulmonary edema', 'fluid overload', 'chf'],
    'Consolidation':    ['consolidation', 'lobar consolidation', 'airspace consolidation'],
    'Pneumonia':        ['pneumonia', 'infection', 'fever', 'sepsis'],
    'Atelectasis':      ['atelectasis', 'collapse', 'subsegmental'],
    'Pneumothorax':     ['pneumothorax', 'ptx', 'chest pain', 'trauma'],
    'Pleural Effusion': ['effusion', 'pleural', 'pleural fluid'],
    'Pleural Other':    ['pleural thickening', 'pleural disease', 'empyema'],
    'Fracture':         ['fracture', 'rib fracture', 'trauma', 'fall'],
    'Support Devices':  ['intubated', 'icu', 'ventilated', 'post-op', 'line', 'tube'],
}

_NEGATION_PREFIXES = (
    'rule out', 'r/o', 'no ', 'without ', 'negative for', 'exclude ',
    'unlikely', 'no evidence', 'no signs', 'no sign of',
)

def infer_likely_labels(indication: str) -> list[str]:
    indication_lower = indication.lower()
    triggered = []
    for label, kws in _INDICATION_KEYWORDS.items():
        for kw in kws:
            idx = indication_lower.find(kw)
            if idx == -1:
                continue
            prefix = indication_lower[max(0, idx - 25):idx]
            if any(neg in prefix for neg in _NEGATION_PREFIXES):
                continue
            triggered.append(label)
            break
    return triggered

def build_association_hint(
    likely_labels: list[str],
    rules_df: pd.DataFrame,
    min_confidence: float = 0.25,
    min_lift: float = 1.2,
    max_rules: int = 4,
) -> str:
    """Return a formatted soft prior, or empty string if no strong rules apply."""
    if not likely_labels:
        return ''
    relevant = rules_df[
        rules_df['antecedent'].isin(likely_labels) &
        (rules_df['confidence'] >= min_confidence) &
        (rules_df['lift'] >= min_lift)
    ].sort_values('confidence', ascending=False).head(max_rules)

    if relevant.empty:
        return ''
    lines = ['Clinical association context (training set statistics):']
    for _, rule in relevant.iterrows():
        lines.append(
            f'  - {rule["antecedent"]} co-occurs with {rule["consequent"]} '
            f'in {100*rule["confidence"]:.0f}% of similar studies '
            f'(lift={rule["lift"]:.2f}).'
        )
    return '\n'.join(lines)

def build_conditioned_prompt(
    indication: str,
    rules_df: pd.DataFrame,
    min_confidence: float = 0.25,
    min_lift: float = 1.2,
    system_prompt: str = '',
) -> str:
    likely_labels = infer_likely_labels(indication)
    rule_hint     = build_association_hint(likely_labels, rules_df, min_confidence, min_lift)

    # TF-IDF label prior overrides rule hint when retrieval succeeds
    priors    = retrieve_label_prior(indication)
    prior_hint = build_label_prior_hint(priors) if priors is not None else ''
    hint = prior_hint if prior_hint else rule_hint

    parts = []
    if system_prompt:
        parts.append(system_prompt)
    if hint:
        parts.append(hint)
        parts.append('')
    if indication.strip():
        parts.append(f'Indication: {indication.strip()}')
    parts.append('Findings:')
    return '\n'.join(parts)

# ── Smoke test ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TEST_CASES = [
    'Shortness of breath, rule out pleural effusion',
    'Chest pain, history of CHF and pulmonary edema',
    'Post-op day 1, patient intubated in ICU',
    'Fever, cough, possible pneumonia',
    'Routine PA and lateral, no specific indication',
]
for ind in TEST_CASES:
    triggered = infer_likely_labels(ind)
    hint      = build_association_hint(triggered, rules_df)
    print(f'=== Indication: "{ind}"')
    print(f'    Triggered labels: {triggered}')
    if hint:
        print(hint)
    else:
        print('    (no strong rules — standard prompt)')
    print()

STEP 5 — Compute and save metrics


Code
import bert_score.utils as _bsu
from bert_score import score as _bert_score
from src.data.labels import run_chexbert
from sklearn.metrics import f1_score
import evaluate as hf_evaluate

_orig_sent_encode = _bsu.sent_encode
def _safe_sent_encode(tokenizer, sent):
    if getattr(tokenizer, 'model_max_length', 0) > 10_000:
        tokenizer.model_max_length = 512
    return _orig_sent_encode(tokenizer, sent)
_bsu.sent_encode = _safe_sent_encode

_bleu  = hf_evaluate.load('bleu')
_rouge = hf_evaluate.load('rouge')

cache_m = REPO_ROOT / 'reports' / f'eval_metrics_{variant_key}.json'
if cache_m.exists():
    cond_metrics = json.loads(cache_m.read_text())
    print(f'Loaded metrics from {cache_m.name}')
else:
    refs = references
    print('BERTScore…')
    _, _, F = _bert_score(cond_hyps, refs,
        model_type=params['eval']['bertscore_model'],
        lang='en', device=DEVICE, verbose=False, batch_size=16)
    print('CheXbert…')
    hyp_labels = run_chexbert(cond_hyps, uncertain_policy='present', device=DEVICE)
    ref_labels = run_chexbert(refs,      uncertain_policy='present', device=DEVICE)
    print('BLEU/ROUGE…')
    cond_metrics = {
        'variant': variant_key,
        'bertscore_f1':      float(F.mean()),
        'chexbert_micro_f1': float(f1_score(ref_labels, hyp_labels, average='micro', zero_division=0)),
        'chexbert_macro_f1': float(f1_score(ref_labels, hyp_labels, average='macro', zero_division=0)),
        'bleu4':             _bleu.compute(predictions=cond_hyps, references=[[r] for r in refs], max_order=4)['bleu'],
        'rouge_l':           _rouge.compute(predictions=cond_hyps, references=refs)['rougeL'],
        'per_label_f1':      {label: float(f1_score(ref_labels[:, i], hyp_labels[:, i], zero_division=0))
                              for i, label in enumerate(CHEXBERT_LABELS)},
        'bertscore_per_study': F.tolist(),
    }
    cache_m.write_text(json.dumps(cond_metrics, indent=2))
    print(f'Saved → {cache_m.name}')

_bsu.sent_encode = _orig_sent_encode

# Load baseline for comparison
baseline_v3_path = REPO_ROOT / 'reports' / 'eval_metrics_uniform_v3.json'
comparison = {}
if baseline_v3_path.exists():
    comparison['uniform_v3'] = json.loads(baseline_v3_path.read_text())
comparison[variant_key] = cond_metrics

DISPLAY_NAMES = {
    'uniform_v3':              'QLoRA uniform (v3) — no conditioner',
    'assoc_rules_uniform_v3':  'Assoc. rules conditioner (v3)',
}

rows = []
for v, m in comparison.items():
    rows.append({'Model': DISPLAY_NAMES.get(v, v),
                 'BERTScore-F1':      round(m['bertscore_f1'],      4),
                 'CheXbert micro-F1': round(m['chexbert_micro_f1'], 4),
                 'CheXbert macro-F1': round(m['chexbert_macro_f1'], 4),
                 'BLEU-4':            round(m['bleu4'],             4),
                 'ROUGE-L':           round(m['rouge_l'],           4)})
display(pd.DataFrame(rows).set_index('Model'))

if 'uniform_v3' in comparison:
    delta = cond_metrics['bertscore_f1'] - comparison['uniform_v3']['bertscore_f1']
    print(f'\nΔ BERTScore vs no-conditioner baseline: {delta:+.4f}')

# Bar chart
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
names  = [DISPLAY_NAMES.get(v, v) for v in comparison]
bscore = [comparison[v]['bertscore_f1'] for v in comparison]
colors = ['#1f77b4', '#ff7f0e']
bars   = ax.bar(names, bscore, color=colors[:len(comparison)], alpha=0.85, edgecolor='white')
for bar, val in zip(bars, bscore):
    ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2, val + 0.001, f'{val:.4f}',
            ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=9)
ax.set_ylim(min(bscore) * 0.98, max(bscore) * 1.04)
ax.set_ylabel('BERTScore-F1')
ax.set_title('Association Rules Conditioner — impact on BERTScore-F1')
ax.tick_params(axis='x', labelrotation=15)
plt.tight_layout()
fig.savefig(FIGURES_DIR / 'eval_assoc_rules_comparison.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
print('Saved eval_assoc_rules_comparison.png'); plt.show()

Checks whether the conditioner helps specifically on the studies where a rule was triggered.


Code

base_scores)[triggered_mask].mean():+.4f}')    print()    print('BERTScore — non-hinted subset:')    print(f'  Baseline (v3)  : {base_scores[~triggered_mask].mean():.4f}')    print(f'  With conditioner: {cond_scores[~triggered_mask].mean():.4f}')    print(f'  Δ              : {(cond_scores - base_scores)[~triggered_mask].mean():+.4f}')    delta_scores = cond_scores - base_scores    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 4))    ax.hist(delta_scores[triggered_mask],  bins=40, alpha=0.7,            label=f'Hinted (n={n_trig})',        color='#ff7f0e')    ax.hist(delta_scores[~triggered_mask], bins=40, alpha=0.7,            label=f'Standard prompt (n={n_notrig})', color='#1f77b4')    ax.axvline(0, color='black', linestyle='--', linewidth=1)    ax.set_xlabel('ΔBERT Score (conditioned − baseline)')    ax.set_ylabel('Count')    ax.set_title('Per-study BERTScore delta — conditioner vs baseline')    ax.legend()    plt.tight_layout()    fig.savefig(FIGURES_DIR / 'eval_assoc_rules_delta.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')    print('Saved eval_assoc_rules_delta.png'); plt.show()

text:
Purpose: Generate test-set reports, compute the full metric stack, and run both domain shift audits. Advanced prompt engineering strategies (RAG, association rules) live in notebooks 05 and 06. Run notebook 04 first — its cached metric files are the input to the grand comparison in STEP 7.
Checkpoints evaluated: - Zero-shot MedGemma 4B-it (no fine-tuning) - qlora_uniform_v3 — uniform sampling, BERTScore=0.7113 - qlora_weighted_v4 — ESS-based weighted sampler, BERTScore=0.7036, macro-F1=0.0635
Execution order across notebooks:

title: 04 — Core Evaluation and Domain Shift Audit
text:
STEP 1 — Environment setup


Code
import os, sys, json, logging
from pathlib import Path

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import torch
import yaml

matplotlib.rcParams['figure.dpi'] = 120

STEP 2 — Generate test-set reports for all fine-tuned variants
Greedy decoding (temperature=0) for reproducibility.

~25–40 min per variant on Ada RTX 4000 (600 studies).

title: 04 — Core Evaluation and Domain Shift Audit
text:
STEP 3 — Full metric stack

BERTScore-F1 (primary) — microsoft/deberta-xlarge-mnli
CheXbert micro/macro F1 (diagnostic, vocabulary-artifact-affected on IU X-ray)
BLEU-4 and ROUGE-L (NLG comparability)

Cached to reports/eval_metrics_{variant}.json — picked up by notebooks 05/06 and STEP 7.


Code
import bert_score.utils as _bsu
from bert_score import score as _bert_score
from src.data.labels import CHEXBERT_LABELS, run_chexbert
from sklearn.metrics import f1_score
import evaluate as hf_evaluate

_orig_sent_encode = _bsu.sent_encode
def _safe_sent_encode(tokenizer, sent):
    if getattr(tokenizer, 'model_max_length', 0) > 10_000:
        tokenizer.model_max_length = 512
    return _orig_sent_encode(tokenizer, sent)
_bsu.sent_encode = _safe_sent_encode

_bleu  = hf_evaluate.load('bleu')
_rouge = hf_evaluate.load('rouge')

def _compute_metrics(hyps, refs, variant, uncertain_policy='present'):
    cache = REPO_ROOT / 'reports' / f'eval_metrics_{variant}.json'
    if cache.exists():
        print(f'  [{variant}] loaded from cache')
        return json.loads(cache.read_text())
    print(f'  [{variant}] BERTScore…')
    _, _, F = _bert_score(hyps, refs,
        model_type=params['eval']['bertscore_model'],
        lang='en', device=DEVICE, verbose=False, batch_size=16)
    print(f'  [{variant}] CheXbert…')
    hyp_labels = run_chexbert(hyps, uncertain_policy=uncertain_policy, device=DEVICE)
    ref_labels = run_chexbert(refs, uncertain_policy=uncertain_policy, device=DEVICE)
    print(f'  [{variant}] BLEU/ROUGE…')
    result = {
        'variant': variant,
        'bertscore_f1':      float(F.mean()),
        'chexbert_micro_f1': float(f1_score(ref_labels, hyp_labels, average='micro', zero_division=0)),
        'chexbert_macro_f1': float(f1_score(ref_labels, hyp_labels, average='macro', zero_division=0)),
        'bleu4':             _bleu.compute(predictions=hyps, references=[[r] for r in refs], max_order=4)['bleu'],
        'rouge_l':           _rouge.compute(predictions=hyps, references=refs)['rougeL'],
        'per_label_f1':      {label: float(f1_score(ref_labels[:, i], hyp_labels[:, i], zero_division=0))
                              for i, label in enumerate(CHEXBERT_LABELS)},
        'bertscore_per_study': F.tolist(),
    }
    cache.write_text(json.dumps(result, indent=2))
    print(f'  [{variant}] saved → {cache.name}')
    return result

metrics = {}
zs_baseline = REPO_ROOT / 'reports' / 'baseline_results.json'
if zs_baseline.exists():
    raw = json.loads(zs_baseline.read_text())
    m = raw['metrics']
    metrics['zero_shot'] = {
        'variant': 'zero_shot',
        'bertscore_f1':       m.get('bertscore_f1', float('nan')),
        'chexbert_micro_f1':  m.get('f1_chexbert_micro_present', float('nan')),
        'chexbert_macro_f1':  m.get('f1_chexbert_macro_present', float('nan')),
        'bleu4':              m.get('bleu4', float('nan')),
        'rouge_l':            m.get('rouge_l', float('nan')),
        'per_label_f1':       m.get('per_label_f1_present', {}),
        'bertscore_per_study': raw.get('bertscore_per_study', []),
    }
    print('[zero_shot] loaded from baseline_results.json')

for variant, hyps in hypotheses.items():
    if variant == 'zero_shot' and 'zero_shot' in metrics:
        continue
    metrics[variant] = _compute_metrics(hyps, references, variant)

_bsu.sent_encode = _orig_sent_encode
print('\nDone. Variants evaluated:', list(metrics.keys()))

title: 04 — Core Evaluation and Domain Shift Audit
section: STEP 6 — Domain Shift Audit: Prevalence Shift (Importance Sampling)
text:
STEP 6 — Domain Shift Audit: Prevalence Shift (Importance Sampling)
Estimates performance under a different label distribution (e.g. higher Pleural Effusion prevalence — plausible for a LATAM deployment). Uses importance-sampling re-weighting:
w_i = π_target(y_i) / π_source(y_i), weighted_score = Σ w_i·score_i / Σ w_i
Dashed lines = ESS < 30 (estimate unreliable).


Code
from src.data.labels import CHEXBERT_LABELS

SHIFT_LABELS = ['Pleural Effusion', 'Atelectasis', 'Pneumonia', 'Consolidation']
test_label_matrix = test_df[CHEXBERT_LABELS].values.astype(float)

def _importance_weights(label_matrix, label_idx, pi_target, pi_source):
    y = label_matrix[:, label_idx]
    return np.where(y == 1,
        pi_target / max(pi_source, 1e-9),
        (1 - pi_target) / max(1 - pi_source, 1e-9)
    ).astype(np.float64)

def _ess(weights):
    w = weights / weights.sum()
    return float(1.0 / (w**2).sum())

pi_targets = np.linspace(0.01, 0.50, 20)
prev_results = {}
for label in SHIFT_LABELS:
    label_idx = CHEXBERT_LABELS.index(label)
    pi_source = float(test_label_matrix[:, label_idx].mean())
    rows = []
    for variant in ('zero_shot', 'uniform_v3', 'weighted_v4'):
        if variant not in metrics: continue
        scores = np.array(metrics[variant].get('bertscore_per_study', []))
        if len(scores) != len(test_label_matrix): continue
        for pi_t in pi_targets:
            w = _importance_weights(test_label_matrix, label_idx, pi_t, pi_source)
            ess_val = _ess(w)
            rows.append({'variant': variant,
                         'target_prevalence': round(float(pi_t), 4),
                         'weighted_bertscore_f1': float(np.average(scores, weights=w)),
                         'ess': ess_val, 'reliable': ess_val >= 30})
    prev_results[label] = pd.DataFrame(rows)
    print(f'{label}: source prevalence = {pi_source:.3f}')

palette = {'zero_shot': '#7f7f7f', 'uniform_v3': '#1f77b4', 'weighted_v4': '#d62728'}
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, len(SHIFT_LABELS), figsize=(5 * len(SHIFT_LABELS), 4), sharey=True)
for ax, label in zip(axes, SHIFT_LABELS):
    df = prev_results[label]
    for variant, color in palette.items():
        vdf = df[df['variant'] == variant]
        r, u = vdf[vdf['reliable']], vdf[~vdf['reliable']]
        if len(r): ax.plot(r['target_prevalence'], r['weighted_bertscore_f1'],
                           color=color, linewidth=2, label=DISPLAY_NAMES.get(variant, variant))
        if len(u): ax.plot(u['target_prevalence'], u['weighted_bertscore_f1'],
                           color=color, linewidth=1, linestyle=':', alpha=0.5)
    ax.set_xlabel(f'Target prevalence of\n{label}')
    ax.set_title(label); ax.legend(fontsize=7); ax.set_ylim(0.60, 0.80)
axes[0].set_ylabel('Weighted BERTScore-F1')
plt.suptitle('Prevalence Shift Audit (dashed = ESS < 30)', fontsize=12)
plt.tight_layout()
fig.savefig(FIGURES_DIR / 'eval_prevalence_shift.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
print('Saved eval_prevalence_shift.png'); plt.show()

text:
STEP 7 — Grand Comparison (all variants)
Auto-discovers every reports/eval_metrics_*.json file — including those produced by notebooks 05 (RAG) and 06 (association rules). Run this cell last, after all other notebooks have completed.
Execution order: 04 STEPs 1-6 → notebook 05 → notebook 06 → this cell.


Code
# ── Friendly display names for all known variant keys ─────────────────────────
ALL_DISPLAY_NAMES = {
    'zero_shot':                   'Zero-shot MedGemma',
    'uniform_v3':                  'QLoRA uniform (v3)',
    'weighted_v4':                 'QLoRA weighted (v4)',
    'rag_k3_uniform_v3':           'RAG k=3 (v3)',
    'rag_k5_uniform_v3':           'RAG k=5 (v3)',
    'assoc_rules_uniform_v3':      'Assoc. rules (v3)',
    'rag_assoc_combined_uniform_v3': 'RAG + Assoc. rules (v3)',
}

# ── Scan all cached metric files ──────────────────────────────────────────────
all_metrics = {}
for f in sorted((REPO_ROOT / 'reports').glob('eval_metrics_*.json')):
    variant = f.stem.replace('eval_metrics_', '')
    data = json.loads(f.read_text())
    all_metrics[variant] = data
    display_name = ALL_DISPLAY_NAMES.get(variant, variant)
    print(f'  {display_name}: BERTScore={data["bertscore_f1"]:.4f}')

if not all_metrics:
    print('No eval_metrics_*.json files found — run STEPs 1–6 first.')
else:
    rows = []
    for variant, m in sorted(all_metrics.items(), key=lambda x: -x[1]['bertscore_f1']):
        rows.append({
            'Model':             ALL_DISPLAY_NAMES.get(variant, variant),
            'BERTScore-F1':      round(m['bertscore_f1'],      4),
            'CheXbert micro-F1': round(m['chexbert_micro_f1'], 4),
            'CheXbert macro-F1': round(m['chexbert_macro_f1'], 4),
            'BLEU-4':            round(m['bleu4'],             4),
            'ROUGE-L':           round(m['rouge_l'],           4),
        })
    grand_df = pd.DataFrame(rows).set_index('Model')
    display(grand_df)

    # ── Grand comparison bar chart ────────────────────────────────────────────
    ordered_variants = [v for v in (
        'zero_shot', 'uniform_v3', 'weighted_v4',
        'rag_k3_uniform_v3', 'rag_k5_uniform_v3',
        'assoc_rules_uniform_v3', 'rag_assoc_combined_uniform_v3',
    ) if v in all_metrics]
    other_variants = [v for v in all_metrics if v not in ordered_variants]
    ordered_variants += other_variants

    names = [ALL_DISPLAY_NAMES.get(v, v) for v in ordered_variants]
    bscore = [all_metrics[v]['bertscore_f1'] for v in ordered_variants]
    palette_grand = ['#7f7f7f', '#1f77b4', '#d62728', '#2ca02c', '#17becf', '#ff7f0e', '#9467bd']
    colors_grand = (palette_grand * 4)[:len(ordered_variants)]

    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(max(8, 2.2 * len(ordered_variants)), 5))
    bars = ax.bar(names, bscore, color=colors_grand, alpha=0.85, edgecolor='white')
    baseline_score = all_metrics.get('zero_shot', {}).get('bertscore_f1')
    if baseline_score:
        ax.axhline(baseline_score, color='black', linestyle='--', linewidth=1,
                   label=f'Zero-shot baseline ({baseline_score:.4f})')
        ax.legend(fontsize=9)
    for bar, val in zip(bars, bscore):
        ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2, val + 0.001, f'{val:.4f}',
                ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=8)
    ax.set_ylim(min(bscore) * 0.97, max(bscore) * 1.05)
    ax.set_ylabel('BERTScore-F1')
    ax.set_title('Grand Comparison — BERTScore-F1 across all variants', fontsize=13)
    ax.tick_params(axis='x', labelrotation=20)
    plt.tight_layout()

import logging

# ── Weighted sampler justification ───────────────────────────────────────────# Compute and display the p_target design choices with their rationale.# This table is intended for direct inclusion in the final report.import numpy as npimport pandas as pdimport matplotlib.pyplot as pltimport yamlfrom src.data.labels import CHEXBERT_LABELSfrom src.training.sampler import build_sample_weightswith open(REPO_ROOT / 'params.yaml') as f:    params_ws = yaml.safe_load(f)train_df_ws = pd.read_parquet(PROCESSED_DIR / 'train.parquet')train_df_ws = train_df_ws[    train_df_ws['findings'].notna() & (train_df_ws['findings'].str.strip() != '')]label_matrix_ws = train_df_ws[CHEXBERT_LABELS].values.astype(float)n_train = len(label_matrix_ws)p_target    = params_ws['sampler']['p_target']weight_clip = params_ws['sampler']['weight_clip']# ── Per-label summary table ───────────────────────────────────────────────────rows = []for i, label in enumerate(CHEXBERT_LABELS):    nat_prev = label_matrix_ws[:, i].mean()    ess = int(round(nat_prev * n_train))    target = p_target.get(label)    ratio = (target / nat_prev) if (target is not None and nat_prev > 0) else 1.0    if ess < 50 and target is not None:        tier = f'Very rare  (ESS<50)   → ×{ratio:.1f}'    elif ess <= 224 and target is not None:        tier = f'Rare       (ESS≤224)  → ×{ratio:.1f}'    else:        tier = 'Common / no correction'    rows.append({        'Label':            label,        'Nat. prev (%)':    round(nat_prev * 100, 2),        'ESS (train set)':  ess,        'p_target':         f'{target:.2f}' if target is not None else 'null (×1.0)',        'Boost ratio':      f'×{ratio:.1f}',        'Tier':             tier,    })sampler_table = pd.DataFrame(rows).sort_values('ESS (train set)')print('=== Weighted sampler p_target design ===\n')print(sampler_table.to_string(index=False))# ── Sampler ESS summary ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────weights_w = build_sample_weights(label_matrix_ws, p_target=p_target, weight_clip=weight_clip)weights_u = build_sample_weights(label_matrix_ws, p_target=None,     weight_clip=weight_clip)ess_w = weights_w.sum()**2 / (weights_w**2).sum()print(f'\nWeighted sampler ESS : {ess_w:.0f} / {n_train} ({100*ess_w/n_train:.1f}% of training set)')print(f'Uniform  sampler ESS : {n_train} / {n_train} (100.0% — no reweighting)')print(f'weight_clip = {weight_clip}×    max weight = {weights_w.max():.2f}    mean weight = {weights_w.mean():.2f}')print(f'\nInterpretation: the weighted sampler sacrifices {100*(1-ess_w/n_train):.0f}% of effective')print(f'sample diversity to over-represent rare-pathology studies.')# ── Weight distribution figure ────────────────────────────────────────────────fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 4))for ax, weights, label_str, color in [    (axes[0], weights_u, 'Uniform (all weights = 1.0)', '#1f77b4'),    (axes[1], weights_w, f'Weighted v4 (ESS={ess_w:.0f}/{n_train})', '#d62728'),]:    w = np.asarray(weights)    bins = np.linspace(w.min() - 0.01, w.max() + 0.01, min(60, len(np.unique(w)) + 1))    ax.hist(w, bins=bins, color=color, alpha=0.85, edgecolor='white', linewidth=0.3)    ax.axvline(w.mean(),   color='black', linestyle='--', lw=1.5, label=f'mean={w.mean():.2f}')    ax.axvline(np.median(w), color='red', linestyle=':',  lw=1.5, label=f'median={np.median(w):.2f}')    ax.set_xlabel('Sample weight')    ax.set_ylabel('Training studies')    ax.set_title(label_str)    ax.legend(fontsize=9)plt.suptitle('Sampler weight distributions — uniform vs importance-weighted (v4)', fontsize=12)plt.tight_layout()fig.savefig(FIGURES_DIR / 'train_sampler_weights_comparison.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')print(f'\nFigure saved to reports/figures/train_sampler_weights_comparison.png')plt.show()

for ax, weights, title, color in [

]:
    w = np.asarray(weights, dtype=np.float64)

    n_bins = max(1, min(60, len(np.unique(w))))

               label=f'mean={w.mean():.2f}')

title: 01 — EDA: Quantitative Distribution Audit
text:
STEP 6 — K_eff and Shannon entropy
Evaluate: how concentrated is the dataset? K_eff = 1 → one label dominates entirely. K_eff = 14 → all equal.


Code
from src.eda.distribution_audit import compute_keff

keff = compute_keff(prevalences)
p = prevalences.values.clip(1e-9)
p_norm = p / p.sum()
entropy = float(-np.sum(p_norm * np.log(p_norm)))

print(f'Shannon entropy H  = {entropy:.3f} nats')
print(f'K_eff = exp(H)      = {keff:.2f}  (out of 14 possible)')
print()
print('Interpretation: the dataset behaves as if there are only')
print(f'{keff:.1f} equally-common labels — heavily skewed toward "No Finding".')

title: Evaluation Modules
text: This page documents the modules used to evaluate the model’s reports. It details the functions that compute standard natural language metrics (BLEU, ROUGE, BERTScore) and clinical classification metrics (CheXbert). It also documents the code that calculates confidence calibration (how well the model’s confidence aligns with actual accuracy) and importance-weighted test evaluation.

title: Evaluation Modules
text:
Module: metrics.py
Located at src/eval/metrics.py.

Purpose
Implements the core metric evaluation stack. It handles natural language generation (NLG) overlap, semantic embedding matching, and clinical classification labelling.


Key Metrics Supported

CheXbert F1: Runs CheXbert on generated vs. reference text, generating 14-dimensional pathology vectors, and computes micro and macro F1 scores.
BERTScore: Computes contextual similarity using a DeBERTa language model (microsoft/deberta-xlarge-mnli). It is robust to vocabulary changes.
BLEU-4 / ROUGE-L: Measures token overlap and common subsequences, used primarily for comparison with existing literature.



Key Functions

compute_all_metrics(predictions: list[str], references: list[str], policy: UncertaintyPolicy) -> dict: Evaluates the generated findings against reference text using the full metric stack.
_patch_bert_score_overflow(): Workaround function that patches bert_score.utils.sent_encode to prevent tokenizer length conversion overflows.

title: Evaluation Modules
text:
Module: calibration.py
Located at src/eval/calibration.py.

Purpose
Measures the calibration gap between the generated report distribution and the reference reports.


Key Calculations

Prevalence Gap: The absolute difference between the prevalence of a pathology in the generated reports vs. the reference reports.
Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD): Measures divergence between the joint multi-label distributions of the generated and reference report datasets.



Key Functions

compute_prevalence_gaps(pred_matrix: np.ndarray, ref_matrix: np.ndarray) -> pd.Series: Computes absolute classification prevalence errors per clinical pathology.
evaluate_multilabel_calibration(pred_matrix: np.ndarray, ref_matrix: np.ndarray) -> dict: Returns the overall MMD metric and divergence statistics.

title: Evaluation Modules
text:
Module: shift_experiment.py
Located at src/eval/shift_experiment.py.

Purpose
Implements the importance-weighted test set evaluation to simulate different epidemiology mixes (prevalence shifts) without requiring distinct test sets.


Key Mathematical Formulations
When re-weighting the test set to represent a target prevalence, each study \(j\) is assigned a test weight \(v_j\). Weighted classification metrics use these weights to compute weighted precision and recall.
To verify the statistical stability of the weighted estimates, we calculate the Effective Sample Size (ESS):
\[ESS = \frac{\left( \sum_{j=1}^N v_j \right)^2}{\sum_{j=1}^N v_j^2}\]


Key Functions

compute_weighted_metrics(predictions: list[str], references: list[str], weights: np.ndarray) -> dict: Calculates weighted classification and BERTScore statistics.
run_prevalence_sweep(predictions: list[str], references: list[str], target_prevalences: list[dict]) -> pd.DataFrame: Sweeps across multiple target prevalence profiles, reporting performance degradation curves along with the corresponding ESS values.

title: Data Pipeline Modules
text: This page documents the code responsible for loading the raw chest X-ray reports, extracting 14 standard disease labels from free-text using a clinical language tool (CheXbert), and dividing the data into training, validation, and test splits. It ensures that multiple images belonging to the same patient are kept in the same split to prevent training leakage.

title: Data Pipeline Modules
text:
Module: load.py
Located at src/data/load.py.

Purpose
Loads the tabular CSV files from the raw dataset, merges them by study identifier (uid), collapses multi-projection images (frontal and lateral views) into grouped lists, and performs integrity checks.


Key Functions

load_reports(raw_dir: Path) -> pd.DataFrame: Reads indiana_reports.csv which contains study free-text columns (Findings, Impression, Indication, Problems).
load_projections(raw_dir: Path) -> pd.DataFrame: Reads indiana_projections.csv linking each study uid to image filenames and their perspective (Frontal or Lateral).
build_study_df(reports: pd.DataFrame, projections: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame: Performs a left-join grouping images by study. Filenames are aggregated into lists in columns frontal and lateral.
validate_integrity(df: pd.DataFrame) -> dict: Audits the merged DataFrame for missing reports or cases without frontal views. It outputs counts but does not drop entries.

title: Data Pipeline Modules
text:
Module: labels.py
Located at src/data/labels.py.

Purpose
Extracts 14 binary clinical labels from free-text report findings using the CheXbert classification model. It manages uncertain findings based on a versioned policy.


Uncertainty Policies

present: Uncertain findings (CheXbert output 3) are mapped to 1 (positive). This is the default policy.
absent: Uncertain findings and unmentioned findings are mapped to 0 (negative). This is used for ablation analysis.



Key Functions

extract_labels_batch(texts: list[str], policy: UncertaintyPolicy) -> np.ndarray: Processes list of findings using CheXbert, returning an \((n, 14)\) binary numpy matrix. It uses token-level batching for high throughput.
_raw_preds_to_binary(preds: np.ndarray, policy: UncertaintyPolicy) -> np.ndarray: Converts the four CheXbert classes (0=blank, 1=positive, 2=negative, 3=uncertain) into binary outputs based on the policy.

title: Data Pipeline Modules
text:
Module: split.py
Located at src/data/split.py.

Purpose
Divides the study cohort into training, validation, and test sets. It prevents patient-level data leakage and preserves the label distributions.


Design Decisions

Study-Level Splitting: Patients can have multiple images in a study. Splitting is done by study uid so that both frontal and lateral views for a study are kept in the same split.
Iterative Multi-label Stratification: We use iterative_train_test_split from scikit-multilearn to ensure the percentage of rare pathologies remains consistent across training, validation, and testing.



Key Functions

split_dataset(df: pd.DataFrame, test_size: float, val_size: float, random_state: int, min_test_count: int) -> Tuple[pd.DataFrame, pd.DataFrame, pd.DataFrame]: Orchestrates the stratification. Ensures the test set has at least min_test_count studies (default 600).
_iterative_split(df: pd.DataFrame, label_matrix: np.ndarray, test_fraction: float, random_state: int) -> Tuple[pd.DataFrame, pd.DataFrame]: Wrapper function for the scikit-multilearn algorithm.

title: 7. Production Scaling
text: This section looks at what happens when we move from research to production. We discuss the speed (latency) and cost of running a 4-billion parameter model in real-time, explain how we can build safety systems that let the AI say “I don’t know” when its confidence is low, and introduce advanced medical metrics that evaluate clinical errors (such as swapping left and right) rather than just looking at sentence similarity.

title: 7. Production Scaling
text:
Advanced Clinical Metrics
While BLEU, ROUGE, and BERTScore measure token overlap and semantic similarity, they do not guarantee clinical accuracy. In production, we propose evaluating:

GREEN (LLM-as-a-Judge): A GPT-4 based metric trained to identify and categorize clinically significant errors (e.g., misidentifying lateralities, missing critical findings, or hallucinating devices).
RadCliQ (Radiology Report Clinical Quality): A metric that combines lexical overlap and clinical entity matching, calibrated directly to match human radiologist error ratings.
RaTEScore: A semantic metric specifically designed for medical report generation, showing robust handling of clinical synonyms and negation detection.

title: 7. Production Scaling
text:
Production Serving Considerations
Serving a 4B parameter model in real-time clinical workflows requires careful infrastructure design:

Latency: Average token generation for 256 tokens on a modern GPU takes ~1-3 seconds. For real-time radiologist dictation assistance, streaming generation tokens via WebSockets is required.
Abstention & Selective Prediction: A clinical model must know when to say “I don’t know.” We propose implementing an abstention threshold based on output token entropy. If the model’s confidence falls below a threshold, the study is automatically escalated to a senior radiologist without AI output.
OOD (Out-of-Distribution) Guardrails: A production pipeline should run our DomainShiftAudit tests on a small batch of incoming hospital data weekly. If the computed image noise or disease prevalence shifts drastically, the system flags a warning to the MLOps team.
Real OOD Validation: A single dataset can never prove generalization. We discuss why validating on a second independent clinical dataset from a separate hospital system is a mandatory prerequisite for clinical deployment.

title: 5. Inference Conditioning
text:
This section explains two advanced techniques we use to improve the model’s reports without having to perform additional expensive training.
First, we use Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), which searches the training database at inference time for chest X-ray reports with similar clinical indications and injects them as examples in the model prompt. This gives the AI local reference points.
Second, we use Soft Label Priors, which analyze training statistics to find which diseases commonly occur together (for example, fluid overload and lung congestion). When the model generates a report, we feed it these statistical likelihoods as hints, guiding it toward writing clinically logical findings.

title: 5. Inference Conditioning
text:
1. Case-Based Retrieval-Augmented Generation (Kang et al. 2025)
When a radiologist interprets a chest radiograph, they often refer to historical cases with similar clinical presentations. To replicate this case-based reasoning, we implement an inference-time Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) strategy based on the work of Kang et al. (2025).

1.1 Vector Indexing & Similarity Matching
Because clinical indications are dense, structured text, we construct a CPU-efficient term frequency-inverse document frequency (TF-IDF) vectorizer over the training indications:
\[TF\text{-}IDF(t, d) = TF(t, d) \times IDF(t)\]
We extract unigrams and bigrams, capping features at 5,000. At inference time, the query indication \(q\) is vectorized, and cosine similarity is computed against all training vectors:
\[\text{similarity}(q, d_i) = \frac{q \cdot d_i}{\|q\| \|d_i\|}\]
We set a similarity threshold of \(0.05\). If matches exist, we select the top-\(k\) (typically \(k=3\) or \(k=5\)) corresponding training reports to act as few-shot clinical contexts.


1.2 Few-Shot Prompt Injection
The retrieved reference indications and findings are formatted and appended to the system prompt, as shown in the following structure:
You are an expert radiologist. Write only the Findings section...
The following are findings from similar studies for reference:
  Example 1 (similarity=0.72):
    Indication: Shortness of breath, history of smoking.
    Findings: Lungs are hyperexpanded with flattened diaphragms...

Indication: Patient with shortness of breath and low oxygen.
Findings:
This grounds the model in target-vocabulary phrasing before generation, helping to bridge the vocabulary mismatch gap.

title: 5. Inference Conditioning
section: 2. Soft Label Priors via Association Rules (Singh et al. 2026)
text:
2. Soft Label Priors via Association Rules (Singh et al. 2026)
Medical findings do not occur in isolation; they follow logical clinical associations. To steer the decoder toward plausible reports without modifying model weights or gathering more data, we introduce soft label priors derived from label association rules, as proposed in Singh et al. (Singh et al. 2026).

2.1 Mining Co-occurrence Statistics
We compute a pairwise joint occurrence matrix from the binary CheXbert training labels (\(L \in \{0, 1\}^{N \times 14}\)):
\[C = \frac{L^T L}{N}\]
For each pair of labels \(A\) and \(B\), we compute the conditional probability (confidence) and lift:
\[P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)} = \frac{C_{A, B}}{\mu_A}\]
\[\text{Lift}(A \rightarrow B) = \frac{P(B \mid A)}{P(B)} = \frac{C_{A, B}}{\mu_A \mu_B}\]
Where \(\mu_A\) represents the empirical prevalence of label \(A\) in the dataset. Rules are sorted by confidence and filtered using thresholds (confidence \(\ge 0.25\), lift \(\ge 1.2\)). The resulting joint statistics are saved to a heatmap.


2.2 Keyword Triggering and Prompt Conditioning
During test inference, we scan the incoming indication text for keywords related to the 14 target pathologies, using negation filtering (e.g., ignoring mentions preceded by “no evidence of” or “rule out”).
If a pathology \(A\) is triggered, we fetch its highest-confidence associations and append them as a structured hint:
Clinical association context (training set statistics):
  - Atelectasis co-occurs with Lung Opacity in 45% of similar studies (lift=1.82).


2.3 Empirical TF-IDF Prior Overrides
To handle complex cases where keywords are insufficient, we implement an alternative prior estimator: we retrieve the top-\(K\) (\(K=20\)) similar training indications via TF-IDF and compute the empirical label prevalence vector directly over this neighborhood. If this retrieval succeeds, the empirical priors are formatted as a direct prompt context, overriding the rule-based hints.

title: 3. Model & QLoRA Config
text: This section describes the AI model itself and how we train it. We use Google’s MedGemma, which consists of a frozen medical eye (image encoder) and a text generator (language decoder). We train only the text generator using a method called QLoRA, which inserts tiny adjustable elements into the model while keeping the main text book frozen in memory. We also use a masking technique to ensure the model is only graded on the clinical findings it generates, not on the inputs it was given.

title: 3. Model & QLoRA Config
text:
MedGemma Foundation Architecture
We utilize google/medgemma-4b-it as our base model. This foundation architecture is composed of:

Vision Encoder: A frozen medical SigLIP model that extracts visual representation vectors directly from chest radiograph projections.
Language Decoder: A Gemma 3 4B instruction-tuned causal language model, which receives concatenated vision tokens and clinical text prompts.






graph LR
    CXR["CXR Image"] --> SigLIP["SigLIP Encoder (Frozen)"]
    Prompt["Clinical Indication Prompt"] --> Tokenizer["Tokenizer"]
    SigLIP --> Concat["Concat Tokens"]
    Tokenizer --> Concat
    Concat --> Gemma["Gemma 3 4B Decoder (QLoRA)"]
    Gemma --> Report["Generated Findings"]

title: 3. Model & QLoRA Config
text:
Low-Rank Adaptation (QLoRA)
To train the model on a consumer GPU provided by Kaggle with limited VRAM (2 x Tesla T4 16GB), we employ QLoRA (Quantized Low-Rank Adaptation). The base model weights are loaded in 4-bit NormalFloat (NF4) double quantization, while low-rank trainable adapter matrices are injected into the attention projection layers.
Mathematically, the forward pass for a projection layer with input \(x \in \mathbb{R}^d\) is given by:
\[h = W_0 x + \Delta W x = W_0 x + \frac{s}{r} B A x\]
Where:

\(W_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}\) represents the frozen base model weights in NF4 representation.
\(A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times d}\) and \(B \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times r}\) are the trainable LoRA adapter matrices.
\(r\) is the LoRA rank (\(r = 16\)).
\(s\) is the scaling factor (\(\alpha = 32\), yielding \(\frac{s}{r} = 2.0\)).

The adapters are applied to the attention modules: \(q\_proj\), \(k\_proj\), \(v\_proj\), and \(o\_proj\). This reduces the trainable parameters to just ~17M (~0.4%) of the 4B parameter model. The vision encoder is kept fully frozen (\(requires\_grad=False\)) to prevent overfitting on the relatively small dataset.

num_epochs: 2

batch_size: 2

title: 1. Introduction & Strategic Thesis
text: This section introduces the core clinical motivation of the project. Chest X-ray models are usually trained on massive datasets from large US hospitals, which contain mostly English reports and a very high percentage of normal X-rays. When trying to deploy these models in Latin American (LATAM) clinics, they face three major changes: different image properties from different X-ray machines, different languages (Spanish instead of English), and different rates of disease. We frame this problem not as a simple translation or accuracy task, but as a systematic domain shift diagnostic challenge.

title: 1. Introduction & Strategic Thesis
text:
Clinical Domain Shift
Developing a multimodal medical vision-language foundation model that generates chest X-ray findings is one of the key challenges in medical AI. However, deploying such a model in a real clinical setting—particularly across distinct geographic regions like Latin America—introduces severe out-of-distribution (OOD) shifts:

Acquisition Shift: Differences in scanner hardware manufacturers, kVp exposure calibrations, and sensor resolutions produce raw image distributions that diverge from the clean datasets used during pre-training.
Prevalence Shift: Regional epidemiological patterns result in different baseline frequencies of pathologies. For example, Latin American public clinics may exhibit higher prevalences of Tuberculosis or Chagas disease compared to standard Western benchmarks.
Language Shift: Pre-trained clinical vision-language models like MedGemma are pre-trained predominantly on English corpora (e.g., MIMIC-CXR). Clinicians in LATAM dictate findings and indications in Spanish.

title: 1. Introduction & Strategic Thesis
text:
The Strategic Thesis
Our thesis is that cross-geographic deployment must be framed as a distributional discrepancy audit. Rather than optimizing a model to achieve maximum accuracy on a single dataset, we construct a reusable diagnostic infrastructure—the DomainShiftAudit class—capable of auditing models under simulated shifts.
By validating this diagnostic protocol on controlled, synthetic perturbations within the Indiana University Chest X-ray (IU CXR) dataset, we ensure that the auditing infrastructure is fully developed and ready to be run on real, local clinical data from day one.

title: ReportCXR
text:
This project is an AI system built for automated chest X-ray interpretation. Instead of simply fine-tuning a general-purpose model, this work addresses a critical, real-world challenge in healthcare AI: domain shift. Medical AI models trained on United States clinical datasets (which are predominantly English and heavily biased towards normal findings) often fail when deployed out of distribution domains, like hospitals in other regions due to differences in scanner equipment, local patient populations (prevalences of disease), and local reporting languages (Sellergren et al. 2025), (Chen et al. 2024).
Here, we build a robust and reproducible clinical vision-language pipeline based on Google’s MedGemma 4B-it. The system is implemented end-to-end, from data preparation and model adaptation to evaluation and experiment tracking. To ensure reproducibility and auditability, the pipeline integrates DVC for dataset and artifact versioning, together with Weights & Biases for experiment tracking, metric logging, and model comparison. In addition, we introduce a Domain Shift Audit Protocol designed to systematically quantify model degradation under simulated shifts in imaging equipment, reporting language, and disease prevalence.
Beyond this baseline pipeline, we implement and evaluate two research-driven extensions inspired by recent work. First, we incorporate a retrieval-augmented generation strategy that conditions the model on clinically similar prior reports (Kang et al. 2025), allowing the system to use case-based context at inference time. Second, we introduce soft label priors derived from association rules between radiological findings and diagnoses, which are injected into the prompting or decoding process to guide the model toward clinically plausible outputs without requiring additional supervised fine-tuning data (Singh et al. 2026).

title: ReportCXR
text:
Project Overview
The ReportCXR project is developed as part of the Eden AI technical challenge for the Applied Research role. The system fine-tunes a medical vision-language model, google/medgemma-4b-it (which combines a frozen medical SigLIP image encoder and a Gemma 3 4B-it language decoder), to generate radiological reports (specifically the Findings section) conditioned on Clinical Indications.

The Strategic Thesis: Domain Shift Auditing
Our central premise is that deploying medical models across geographies is a distributional shift problem. Rather than reporting static accuracy metrics on a clean dataset, this project designs and validates a reusable diagnostic infrastructure—the DomainShiftAudit framework—on simulated shifts inside the Indiana University Chest X-ray (IU CXR) dataset.
This protocol acts as a diagnostic suite. It measures the robustness of models before they go live:

Acquisition Shift: Simulates older equipment or different scanner manufacturers by applying synthetic noise, contrast adjustments, and compression.
Language Shift: Assesses how a model trained in English performs when prompting and generating in Spanish.
Prevalence Shift: Simulates different disease rates using importance sampling to evaluate how the model handles a LATAM patient distribution.

title: ReportCXR
text:
Website Structure
Use the navigation menu at the top to explore the work:

Technical Report: A modular, multi-part deep dive into the strategic thesis, data distribution, model training, evaluation metrics, domain shift experiments, and production scaling.
Code Documentation: Detailed module-by-module documentation of every component in the codebase (data loading, label extraction, QLoRA training, metric evaluation, and synthetic shifts).
Notebooks: Step-by-step interactive implementations of our entire pipeline (EDA, baseline zero-shot, training, and evaluation).

title: ReportCXR
text:
MLOps Setup and Reproducibility
This project is built for complete mathematical reproducibility. Data, parameters, and pipelines are versioned.

1. Versioned Parameters (params.yaml)
Every parameter (from QLoRA rank to target sampler disease prevalences) is declared in a single source of truth: params.yaml. No thresholds or weights are hardcoded inside the code in src/.


2. DVC Pipeline (dvc.yaml)
The data pipeline is managed via DVC (Data Version Control). To reproduce the pipeline stages, run:
# Verify status of data and steps
dvc status

# Reproduce the entire pipeline (loading -> labeling -> splitting -> training -> evaluation)
dvc repro
The pipeline contains the following stages defined in dvc.yaml:

load: Joins reports and projections CSVs and links images.
labels: Processes free-text findings with a CheXbert labeler to build a 14-dimensional pathology matrix.
split: Employs iterative multi-label stratification to split studies cleanly without patient leakage.
eda: Computes prevalences and class statistics, outputting training distribution plots.
train_uniform: Trains QLoRA on a uniform distribution.
train_weighted: Trains QLoRA with active sampling weights.
eval: Computes test metrics on the best saved models.



3. Experiment Tracking (Weights and Biases)
All training runs, validation curves, and sampler weight distributions are synced to Weights and Biases under the project reportcxr.

title: 2. Data Distribution & Sampler Correction
text: This section explains how we handle the huge imbalance in clinical data. Most X-rays are completely normal, meaning that an AI trained on this data would quickly learn to default to predicting “normal” to get easy high scores. We solve this by calculating mathematical weights for each study. When the AI trains, it is shown rare pathologies more frequently and normal X-rays less frequently. This forces the model to actually learn the signatures of rare diseases rather than taking lazy shortcuts.

title: 2. Data Distribution & Sampler Correction
text:
Dataset Distribution
The Indiana University Chest X-ray (IU CXR) dataset is heavily skewed toward normal findings. Out of ~3,300 training studies, ~56% contain “No Finding.” Rare diseases (such as Pneumothorax or Consolidation) account for less than 1% of the dataset.











(a) Label Prevalences in the IU CXR Dataset











(b) Pathology Co-occurrence Matrix













(c) Effective Sample Size (ESS) per Clinical Label







Figure 1: Clinical data distributions and label statistics.



The severe class imbalance is illustrated in Figure 1 (a) and Figure 1 (b). To further quantify the representation of rare pathologies, we compute the Effective Sample Size (ESS) per class (see Figure 1 (c)).

title: 2. Data Distribution & Sampler Correction
text:
Importance Sampling & Sampler Correction
To prevent the model from collapsing to the majority class and simply predicting “normal” for all patients, we implement a WeightedRandomSampler. For each study \(j\), we compute an importance weight \(w_j\) based on the target prevalences specified in params.yaml:
\[w_j = \text{clip}\left( \left( \prod_{l \in L_j} \frac{p_{\text{target}}(l)}{p_{\text{dataset}}(l)} \right)^{\frac{1}{|L_j|}}, \text{clip\_val} \right)\]
Where:

\(L_j\) is the set of positive pathologies present in study \(j\).
\(p_{\text{target}}(l)\) is the user-specified target prevalence for pathology \(l\).
\(p_{\text{dataset}}(l)\) is the empirical prevalence of pathology \(l\) in the training set.
\(\text{clip\_val}\) is the clipping multiplier (sampler.weight_clip = 10.0) to avoid high-variance gradients from extremely rare combinations.

For studies with no positive pathologies (“No Finding”), we assign a base weight:
\[w_j = \frac{p_{\text{target}}(\text{No Finding})}{p_{\text{dataset}}(\text{No Finding})}\]
Applying these weights at the batch-selection level forces the model to encounter rare pathology patterns during backpropagation without modifying the loss function, avoiding gradient-scale instabilities.

ESS-Based Sampler Settings (v4)
In the v4 training run, we configured the target prevalences \(p_{\text{target}}\) in params.yaml directly using the class Effective Sample Size (ESS) metrics computed in Figure 1 (c):

Common Pathologies (ESS > 250): No correction needed. Target prevalences set to null, defaulting to raw dataset prevalence (e.g. Enlarged Cardiomediastinum, Cardiomegaly, Lung Opacity, No Finding).
Moderate Pathologies (ESS 50–224): Boosted to a 10% target prevalence (e.g. Atelectasis, Support Devices, Lung Lesion, Fracture, Pleural Effusion).
Rare Pathologies (ESS < 50): Aggressively boosted to a 5% target prevalence (e.g. Pleural Other, Edema, Pneumonia, Consolidation, Pneumothorax).

This structured up-weighting guarantees that the backpropagated gradients contain meaningful representations of rare clinical conditions, preventing the training loop from defaulting to the dominant “normal” mode.

title: 4. Evaluation & Metric Pivot
text: This section highlights our most important scientific finding. Initially, our automated grader (CheXbert) showed that the model’s pathology scores collapsed to zero after training. However, by reading the actual reports generated by the VLLM, we discovered that the AI was performing excellently. It was describing pathologies in clinical terms (e.g., “enlarged heart”) instead of shorthand terms (e.g., “cardiomegaly”). CheXbert was too rigid to match these, leading us to pivot to BERTScore, which evaluates semantic meaning rather than literal vocabulary.

title: 4. Evaluation & Metric Pivot
text:
Baseline Zero-Shot Evaluation
Before training, we establish the performance of the raw google/medgemma-4b-it model on the 600-study test set under zero-shot prompting. This represents our baseline performance.
Zero-Shot Baseline Results (reports/baseline_results.json):

BERTScore-F1: \(0.6938\)
BLEU-4: \(0.0957\)
ROUGE-L: \(0.2635\)
CheXbert micro-F1 (U\(\rightarrow\)present): \(0.3967\)
CheXbert macro-F1 (U\(\rightarrow\)present): \(0.1416\)

title: 4. Evaluation & Metric Pivot
text:
The Metric Pivot: CheXbert \(\rightarrow\) BERTScore
During Phase 4 (v1 and v2 training), we encountered a critical anomaly that led to a major architectural pivot.

The Macro-F1 Collapse Anomaly
In v1 and v2 training, we optimized the checkpoint selection callback to save the model that achieved the highest CheXbert micro-F1. However, after fine-tuning, the macro-F1 collapsed by ~57% (dropping from \(0.1416\) zero-shot to \(0.0400\) fine-tuned), even though training loss showed a healthy monotonic decay.
We formulated two competing hypotheses:

H1 (Training Collapse): The model suffered from catastrophic forgetting and learned to output a static “normal” report for all cases.
H2 (Metric Mismatch / Vocabulary Shift): The model is generating clinically accurate findings but using local IU X-ray vocabulary that the CheXbert labeler (which was trained on Boston-based MIMIC-CXR reports) cannot parse.



Verification via Spot-Check
We loaded the fine-tuned adapter and ran qualitative generations on validation samples (Notebook 03, STEP 7b). The results confirmed H2:









Case
Ground Truth Pathologies
Model Generated Report
CheXbert Evaluation




Case 3
Emphysema, LLL airspace disease
“Hyperexpanded lungs with flattened diaphragms, opacification in the left lower lobe”
No Finding (Failure)


Case 4
Cardiomegaly, Congestion
“Enlarged cardiac silhouette, bilateral airspace disease, bilateral pleural effusions”
No Finding (Failure)



CheXbert was trained on specific MIMIC-CXR terms (e.g., “emphysema,” “cardiomegaly”). IU X-ray reports (which the model learned to emulate) describe these findings descriptively (e.g., “hyperexpanded lungs with flattened diaphragms” for emphysema; “enlarged cardiac silhouette” for cardiomegaly). Because CheXbert looks for rigid clinical keyword mappings, it labeled the model’s highly accurate descriptive reports as “No Finding”, dragging the classification macro-F1 to zero.


The Pivot: BERTScore-F1 Checkpointing
To resolve this, we pivoted to BERTScore-F1 (using microsoft/deberta-xlarge-mnli embeddings) as our primary checkpoint selection metric. BERTScore measures semantic contextual similarity. Because “hyperexpanded lungs” and “emphysema” map to the same clinical semantic space, BERTScore correctly rewards the model for accurate descriptions regardless of exact vocabulary. CheXbert F1 was demoted to a diagnostic metric only.

(a) Training Loss Curve (Uniform vs. Weighted)











(b) Validation Metrics over Epochs













(c) Distribution of Sampler Weights during Training







Figure 1: Comparison of training dynamics and sampling weights.




Results Analysis

Semantic Improvement: All fine-tuned models outperform the zero-shot base model on BERTScore-F1 (as shown in Figure 1 (b)), indicating that fine-tuning successfully aligned MedGemma to the reporting style of the target dataset.
Uniform (v3) vs. Weighted (v4): The uniform sampler (v3) achieved the highest overall semantic quality (\(0.7113\) BERTScore-F1). However, the ESS-based weighted sampler (v4) resolved the severe pathology collapse, achieving a +58% relative improvement in macro-F1 (\(0.0635\) vs. \(0.0401\) in v3) at a minor cost of \(-0.77\%\) BERTScore-F1. This makes v4 the preferred variant for clinical deployment settings prioritizing rare-pathology recall (e.g., triage workflows).
Convergence and Sampler Impact: Uniform fine-tuning (v3) peaked at Epoch 1 and regressed in Epoch 2, showing that the model quickly fit the dominant “normal” mode. In contrast, the weighted sampler (v4) peaked at Epoch 2. Oversampling rare pathology studies prevented the model from quickly collapsing to the majority class, extending the useful training window and enabling more stable convergence.
Sampler Verification: The weight distribution shown in Figure 1 (c) confirms that active weight clipping functions correctly, preventing single outlier studies from dominating the gradient update steps.

title: 6. Domain Shift Audit Protocol
text: This section outlines our system stress-testing infrastructure. Rather than hoping the model works when moved to a new hospital, we write a diagnostic program that simulates three types of shifts: scanner machinery changes (adding noise and changing brightness/contrast), language transitions (evaluating Spanish generations), and epidemiological changes (simulating different rates of diseases). We leave placeholders for the exact degradation curves, as this represents our testing protocol.

title: 6. Domain Shift Audit Protocol
text:
Overview of the Audit Protocol
To evaluate the system’s readiness for Latin American clinical environments, we construct the DomainShiftAudit suite. This module simulates the three core axes of distributional shift.

title: 6. Domain Shift Audit Protocol
text:
Axis A: Acquisition & Equipment Shift
We apply controlled synthetic image perturbations to simulate differences in scanner hardware, sensor noise, or digitization quality (e.g., older equipment in LATAM public hospitals). We sweep over: * Gaussian Noise: Adding noise with standard deviation \(\sigma \in [0, 50]\) in pixel units. * Contrast Scaling: Factors \(c \in [0.5, 2.0]\). * Gamma Correction: \(\gamma \in [0.5, 2.0]\) (simulating exposure discrepancies). * JPEG Compression: Qualities \(q \in [95, 75, 50, 25, 10]\) (simulating network bandwith saving compression).
Space left for remaining experimental degradation curves.

title: 6. Domain Shift Audit Protocol
text:
Axis B: Language Shift
Since MedGemma was pre-trained primarily on English medical text, deploying it in a Spanish-speaking hospital represents a severe vocabulary shift. We test the model’s Spanish generation by prompting it to output findings in Spanish and evaluate the output against automatic Spanish translations of the ground-truth findings.
Space left for remaining language degradation metrics.

title: 6. Domain Shift Audit Protocol
text:
Axis C: Prevalence Shift
Medical centers in LATAM exhibit different prevalence distributions of diseases compared to US datasets (e.g., higher rates of Tuberculosis or Chagas). We simulate this shift by re-weighting our test set using importance sampling. We compute weighted test metrics and report the Effective Sample Size (ESS) to ensure our estimators remain statistically stable:
\[ESS = \frac{\left( \sum_{j=1}^N w_j \right)^2}{\sum_{j=1}^N w_j^2}\]
If the \(ESS\) falls below a safety threshold (e.g., \(ESS < 50\)), we flag the evaluation as statistically unreliable due to extreme distribution divergence.
Space left for remaining importance-weighted test curves.

title: Code Architecture Overview
text: This page describes the organization of the code repository. We explain what each folder does, show how data flows through our modular Python files, and discuss why we avoid writing all of our logic in single, massive Jupyter Notebooks. Instead, we write clean, reusable Python scripts, using notebooks only to orchestrate and visualize results.

title: Code Architecture Overview
text:
Directory Structure
The codebase is organized into modules to ensure a strict separation of concerns. All core logic resides in src/, while notebooks are strictly reserved for orchestration and visualization:
ReportCXR/
│
├── params.yaml                    # Single source of truth for all parameters
├── dvc.yaml                       # Pipeline stages and dependency tracking
├── pyproject.toml                 # Package configuration
│
├── src/
│   ├── data/                      # Data downloading, processing, splitting
│   ├── eda/                       # Distribution auditing
│   ├── training/                  # Model loading, sampling, training loops
│   ├── eval/                      # Metrics and evaluation scripts
│   └── domain_shift_audit/        # Stress-testing infrastructure
│
├── notebooks/                     # Orchestration notebooks
└── reports/                       # Generated figures, logs, baseline JSONs

title: Code Architecture Overview
text:
Modular Design Principles

Logic in Python Modules: We avoid monolithic notebooks. Scripts in src/ are imported into notebooks. This allows for unit testing, easier git diffing, and cleaner refactoring.
Versioned Configuration: All numerical constants, paths, and hyperparameters are housed in params.yaml. Scripts read parameters at runtime, preventing silent configuration drift.
Pipeline Reproducibility: The dvc.yaml file defines explicit data lineage. This ensures that change in code or parameter will trigger only the necessary downstream stages.
Experiment Tracking: The HuggingFace trainer logs to Weights & Biases automatically. This logs the complete history of training losses, learning rates, validation BERTScore, and CheXbert classification F1.

title: Training Pipeline Modules
text: This page documents the components used to configure, sample, and train the AI model. We explain the modules that load the MedGemma weights in a compressed format, compute the study sampling weights for balancing, build training prompts, and run the training loop. It also documents the callback that evaluates the model after every epoch and saves the best version based on semantic score.

title: Training Pipeline Modules
text:
Module: model.py
Located at src/training/model.py.

Purpose
Handles loading google/medgemma-4b-it from Hugging Face with 4-bit NF4 quantization, sets up the Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) LoRA configurations, and constructs prompt structures.


Key Functions

load_model_and_processor(model_id: str, quantization: str, device_map: str) -> Tuple[Any, Any]: Loads the model and the associated image/text processor. Initializes BitsAndBytesConfig for 4-bit NF4 representation.
apply_qlora(model: Any, lora_config: dict) -> Any: Injects trainable LoRA matrices into attention projections (q_proj, k_proj, v_proj, o_proj) with configured rank, alpha, and dropout.
build_prompt(indication: str) -> str: Formats clinical context into MedGemma chat template format.
_best_attn_impl() -> str: Selects the optimal attention implementation (flash_attention_2, sdpa, or eager) depending on local package support.

title: Training Pipeline Modules
text:
Module: sampler.py
Located at src/training/sampler.py.

Purpose
Calculates per-study training weights based on target prevalences and instantiates WeightedRandomSampler instances.


Key Functions

build_sample_weights(label_matrix: np.ndarray, p_target: dict, weight_clip: float) -> np.ndarray: Computes the geometric mean of importance ratios for positive labels on each study:
\[w_j = \left( \prod_{l \in L_j} \frac{p_{\text{target}}(l)}{p_{\text{dataset}}(l)} \right)^{\frac{1}{|L_j|}}\]
Weights are clipped at weight_clip × mean_weight to prevent high gradient variance.
build_sampler(weights: np.ndarray) -> WeightedRandomSampler: Returns a PyTorch sampler that selects batch items based on the calculated weight distribution.

title: Training Pipeline Modules
text:
Module: train.py
Located at src/training/train.py.

Purpose
Orchestrates the SFT (Supervised Fine-Tuning) loop using the HuggingFace Trainer interface. It manages datasets, prompt collators, and checkpoint callbacks.


Key Classes & Functions

CXRReportDataset(Dataset): PyTorch dataset. Loads parquet split data and pairs each study with its corresponding chest X-ray image.
make_collate_fn(processor: Any, max_len: int) -> Callable: Collator function that tokenizes the text prompt and full generation, builds the completion mask by setting label indices before the Findings token length to -100, and handles fallback blank gray images when frontal images are missing.
F1CheckpointCallback(TrainerCallback): Runs greedy validation text generation at the end of each training epoch, computes DeBERTa BERTScore-F1 and CheXbert classification scores, logs metrics to Weights & Biases, and saves the adapter checkpoint if BERTScore improves.
CXRTrainer(Trainer): Extension of HuggingFace Trainer to inject the custom WeightedRandomSampler into the training dataloader.
plot_training_figures(...): Automatically plots training loss decay, validation metric curves, and sampler weight distributions.

title: Domain Shift Audit Modules
text: This page documents the Stress-Testing and Audit suite. The codebase packages individual stress-tests (scanner noise, Spanish translation evaluation, and epidemiological shifts) under a single, unified class called DomainShiftAudit. This class acts as a reusable tool. Instead of running separate experiments, developers can run this audit tool on any model-data pair to analyze degradation behavior before deployment.

title: Domain Shift Audit Modules
text:
Module: audit.py
Located at src/domain_shift_audit/audit.py.

Purpose
Acts as the central orchestrator for the domain shift audit. It unifies the three sub-experiments under a common interface:
auditor = DomainShiftAudit(model, processor)
result = auditor.audit(dataset, images, shift_type='acquisition', perturb_type='gaussian_noise')


Key Classes

ShiftAuditResult: Dataclass container returning:

shift_type: Axis probed (acquisition, language, or prevalence).
degradation_curve: DataFrame containing columns [magnitude, metric, relative_degradation].
ess: Effective Sample Size series (populated for prevalence sweeps).
ci_low / ci_high: Bootstrap confidence intervals.

DomainShiftAudit: The runner class. Orchestrates the pipeline and delegates execution to specialized shift modules depending on the configuration.

title: Domain Shift Audit Modules
text:
Module: acquisition_shift.py
Located at src/domain_shift_audit/acquisition_shift.py.

Purpose
Simulates scanner-level perturbations on input images to evaluate visual encoder robustness under hardware discrepancies.


Supported Perturbations

perturb_brightness(img, factor): Scales pixel intensities.
perturb_contrast(img, factor): Adjusts contrast scaling.
perturb_gamma(img, gamma): Power-law correction \(I_{out} = (I_{in}/255)^{\gamma} \times 255\).
perturb_gaussian_noise(img, std): Adds zero-mean Gaussian noise.
perturb_jpeg_compression(img, quality): Performs lossy compression.



Key Functions

run_acquisition_sweep(images: list, perturb_type: PerturbType, magnitudes: list, metric_fn: Callable) -> AcquisitionSweepResult: Iterates over magnitudes, applies image transforms, runs the metric evaluation function, and compiles the degradation curve.

title: Domain Shift Audit Modules
text:
Module: language_shift.py
Located at src/domain_shift_audit/language_shift.py.

Purpose
Evaluates MedGemma’s generation when prompting and outputting reports in Spanish.


Key Functions

run_language_shift(model: Any, processor: Any, dataset: pd.DataFrame, target_language: str) -> dict: Evaluates predictions generated in Spanish against translated reference findings. It reports BLEU, ROUGE, and BERTScore variations relative to the English baseline.

title: Domain Shift Audit Modules
text:
Module: prevalence_shift.py
Located at src/domain_shift_audit/prevalence_shift.py.

Purpose
Re-envisions the importance-weighted test set sweep as an audit of model robustness under regional epidemiological changes (e.g., target LATAM clinical environments).


Key Functions

run_prevalence_audit(predictions: list, references: list, label_matrix: np.ndarray, target_scenarios: list[dict]) -> dict: Evaluates model degradation across clinical scenarios, matching predictions against simulated target disease prevalences, and tracking ESS.

title: 02 — Baseline: MedGemma 4B-it Zero-Shot
text:
Purpose: Establish the pre-fine-tuning performance anchor.
Every subsequent result (fine-tuned model, shift-corrected sampler) is compared to these numbers.
Protocol (MedGemma 1.5 Technical Report §3.1): - Temperature = 0.0 (greedy decoding — no sampling variance) - No few-shot examples (pure zero-shot) - Metrics computed under both uncertainty policies: present and absent
Steps:
Requires: GPU with ≥ 16 GB VRAM (T4 / L4 / A10G).
Code

title: 02 — Baseline: MedGemma 4B-it Zero-Shot
text:
STEP 3 — Zero-shot generation

Each test study → frontal CXR image + indication → generated Findings text
Temperature = 0 (greedy, do_sample=False)
Hypotheses saved to data/processed/zero_shot_hypotheses.txt — skip inference on re-run

Evaluate: do the generated reports look clinically plausible?


Code
from PIL import Image

SYSTEM_PROMPT = (
    'You are an expert radiologist. '
    'Write only the Findings section of a radiology report for the chest X-ray shown. '
    'Be concise and clinical. Do not include an Impression section.'
)


def _build_messages(indication: str | None, has_image: bool) -> list:
    content = []
    if has_image:
        content.append({'type': 'image'})
    user_text = SYSTEM_PROMPT
    if indication and indication.strip() and indication.strip().lower() not in {'nan', 'none'}:
        user_text = f'Indication: {indication.strip()}\n{SYSTEM_PROMPT}'
    content.append({'type': 'text', 'text': user_text})
    return [{'role': 'user', 'content': content}]


@torch.inference_mode()
def generate_report(row: pd.Series) -> str:
    """Generate a zero-shot findings report for one test study."""
    # Load frontal image
    pil_image = None
    frontal_files = row.get('frontal', [])
    if isinstance(frontal_files, list) and frontal_files:
        img_path = IMAGES_DIR / frontal_files[0]
        if img_path.exists():
            try:
                pil_image = Image.open(img_path).convert('RGB')
            except Exception:
                pil_image = None

    indication = str(row.get('indication', '')) if pd.notna(row.get('indication')) else ''
    messages   = _build_messages(indication, has_image=pil_image is not None)

    # Tokenize via chat template
    text = processor.apply_chat_template(messages, add_generation_prompt=True, tokenize=False)
    proc_kwargs = dict(text=text, return_tensors='pt', padding=True)
    if pil_image is not None:
        proc_kwargs['images'] = [pil_image]
    inputs = processor(**proc_kwargs)
    inputs = {k: v.to(model.device) for k, v in inputs.items()}

    input_len = inputs['input_ids'].shape[1]
    outputs   = model.generate(
        **inputs,
        max_new_tokens=params['model']['max_new_tokens'],
        do_sample=False,           # greedy = temperature 0
        pad_token_id=processor.tokenizer.eos_token_id,
    )
    new_tokens = outputs[0][input_len:]
    return processor.tokenizer.decode(new_tokens, skip_special_tokens=True).strip()


print('generate_report() defined. Ready to run inference.')


generate_report() defined. Ready to run inference.




Code
from tqdm.auto import tqdm

if HYP_FILE.exists():
    print(f'Loading cached hypotheses from {HYP_FILE}')
    hypotheses = HYP_FILE.read_text().splitlines()
    assert len(hypotheses) == len(test_df), (
        f'Cache mismatch: {len(hypotheses)} lines vs {len(test_df)} test studies. '
        f'Delete {HYP_FILE} and re-run to regenerate.'
    )
    print(f'Loaded {len(hypotheses)} hypotheses.')
else:
    print(f'Running zero-shot inference on {len(test_df)} studies ...')
    hypotheses = []
    for _, row in tqdm(test_df.iterrows(), total=len(test_df), desc='generating'):
        hyp = generate_report(row)
        # Newlines break the line-per-study format — collapse to space
        hypotheses.append(hyp.replace('\n', ' '))

    HYP_FILE.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    HYP_FILE.write_text('\n'.join(hypotheses))
    print(f'Saved {len(hypotheses)} hypotheses to {HYP_FILE}')

references = test_df['findings'].str.strip().str.replace('\n', ' ', regex=False).tolist()
print(f'\nHypotheses: {len(hypotheses)}  |  References: {len(references)}')

from src.data.labels import run_chexbert
